Commodity Intelligence

Monthly export volume trend by grain type and seasonal loading patterns, Lower Mississippi River, 2025.

Soybean peak: Jan–Mar (South American off-season demand) Corn peak: Apr–Aug (post-US harvest flush) Wheat: Mar–May (HRW/SRW pre-harvest marketing) YSB+DDGS: specialty terminals year-round

Quality & Certification Intelligence

Export quality and stow factor data by grain and grade — derived from inspection certificates issued at Mississippi River ports, 2025. Stow factor: SF (ft³/LT) = 2788.08 / test weight (lb/bu); untrimmed = SF × 1.06. Orange: light grain >52 ft³/LT Blue: heavy <47 ft³/LT

Vessel & Cargo Analytics

Fleet-wide cargo size distribution, queue wait, and loading duration across all Lower Mississippi terminals, 2025.

Vessel Class Reference
ClassCargo RangeTypical Trade
Handymax25,000–39,999 MTShort-haul, Caribbean
Supramax40,000–57,999 MTPanamax lanes
Panamax58,000–79,999 MTTransoceanic bulk
Capesize80,000+ MTLong-haul, Asia

Lower Miss. median: ~50,000 MT (Panamax/Supramax dominant). Midstream float terminals (MGMT, Cooper, Wood Buoys) average Handymax.

Queue wait = ETA first appearance → first LOADING date, capped at 30 days per voyage.

Load duration = median consecutive LOADING report days per vessel visit.

Anchorage & Transit Intelligence

Vessel movement data across the Lower Mississippi approach: time at anchor before berthing, congestion patterns by location, and seasonal trends — 2025.

158.9h Avg Total Port Time
62.1h Avg Anchor Wait
66.5h Avg Terminal Time
26.4h Avg Transit Time
Key Insight: Vessels spend approximately 39% of total port time waiting at anchor before reaching a berth — a critical factor in demurrage planning and vessel scheduling windows.

Seasonal pattern: Peak congestion typically Feb–Mar coincides with South American soybean harvest window and pre-planting US corn-belt barge fleet buildup. Summer low (Jun–Jul) reflects reduced harvest pressure and lower river traffic density.

Key Anchorage Locations — 2025 Activity

Anchorage2025 VisitsAvg WaitMedian WaitNotes

12 Mile Anch64532.8h8.4hInbound approach queue

Pt Celeste Anch21045.6h7.2hRiver staging zone

River Conditions & Seasonal Context

Gauge height and navigation context at key Lower Mississippi River measurement points — historical monthly averages.

Navigation Implications
High water (Mar–May): Accelerated current (3–5 kt vs. 1–2 kt at low water) increases fuel consumption for upstream movements and can restrict draft near Old River Control.

Low water (Jul–Oct): Draft restrictions may apply below Carrollton. Sub-zero gauge readings can suspend loaded barge operations.

Optimal loading window: Dec–Feb at most terminals — moderate stage, good visibility, pre-South American harvest demand peak.
Fog & Visibility
New Orleans averages 34 fog days/year, concentrated Nov–Mar. River fog closures (Coast Guard VTS) typically last 2–8 hours and primarily affect the Lower Reach (Pilot Town → MM 30). Terminal operations above MM 57 are rarely impacted for more than one tidal cycle.

Seasonal Export Activity — Grain Type Intensity

GrainJanFebMarAprMay JunJulAugSepPeak Window
Corn2.923.153.423.323.243.402.672.913.58Apr–Aug
Soybeans3.222.002.461.330.800.791.061.442.39Jan–Mar
Wheat0.220.280.380.340.270.250.550.600.37Mar–May

Soybean loading peaks Jan–Mar (Southern Hemisphere off-season). Corn dominates Apr–Aug. Wheat exports strongest Mar–May ahead of US harvest.

Barge Origin Corridors

Approximately 55–60% of grain exported via the Lower Mississippi arrives by inland barge from key production corridors. The map shows primary tributary flows converging at the Lower Mississippi grain complex.

CorridorEst. %Primary Grains
Upper Mississippi R.38%Corn, Soybeans
Ohio River20%Corn, Soybeans, Wheat
Illinois River12%Corn, Soybeans
Missouri River12%Corn, Wheat, Soybeans
Arkansas River8%Soybeans, Wheat, Sorghum
Tennessee / Cumberland5%Corn, Soybeans
Local (LA / MS)5%Soybeans, Rice
55–60%
of Lower Miss. export grain
arrives via inland barge
